THE 2060 SETTLEMENT ATLAS

Where humanity can live in a worst-case world

This map models where people could sustainably settle by 2060 under a deliberately harsh scenario: +3°C warming, AMOC collapse, +0.55m seas, no fossil fuels, no synthetic fertilizer, minimal trade. Population isn't assigned from the top down — it's derived from the ground up, climate to water to foodshed to carrying capacity.

The premise is simple and unforgiving: people have to live where the food and water are. When settlements outgrow their local foodshed, the shortfall gets hauled in over long supply chains — and those chains are what breed the exploitation, resource wars, and environmental ruin this scenario refuses to lean on. Strip away the fossil-fuelled logistics that paper over the mismatch, and geography reasserts itself. Every population here is one the surrounding land can actually feed.

Every scrap of land belongs to exactly one of 160 zones: settled regions (R) where people live, excluded zones (X) emptied by heat or flood, and wildlands (W) — two of them true covenant forest left wild by choice, thirteen simply too hostile to hold.

Toggle the layers to explore: cities by fate, drowned coasts, national parks held through the contraction, 253 sacred sites across four lenses, toxic exclusions, and the 2060 refugia where the last great animals flee — onto the same wild land we couldn't use. Click anywhere to identify a zone.

A speculative worldbuilding project · data is modeled, not predicted
